segments.title: The contested Saint-class label
segments.summary: The familiar claim that the G3s would have been the Saint class is set against the Royal Navy's very limited use of saint names.
segments.body: This opening examines why the label is striking rather than self-evident. Apart from HMS Saint George and much older examples, Dr. Clarke finds little Royal Navy precedent for a run of saint-named ships, making the claim worth testing against the naming record.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces the Saint-class claim and the sparse Royal Navy precedent for saint names.

segments.title: Saint George and Saint David are not a class list
segments.summary: The surviving name associations place Saint David and Saint George with different proposed ships, while Saint Patrick carried political complications.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke distinguishes scattered suggestions from evidence for a coordinated class name. Saint David appears more often with the N3s and Saint George with the G3s, but they do not appear as a settled naming set; Saint Patrick would also have been politically awkward during Ireland's break with Britain.

evidence.note: The speaker contrasts the reported G3 and N3 associations and explains the Saint Patrick difficulty.

segments.title: Why admiral names fit the G3 and N3 lineage
segments.summary: Existing Admiral-class paperwork and the later Nelson and Rodney names point to an established Royal Navy naming tradition rather than a sudden Saint class.
segments.body: The video traces names such as Rodney and Nelson through existing capital-ship plans and the Admiral-class theme. It also notes that a naval naming committee was not simply an obedient extension of the First Lord, so an attributed ministerial preference is not proof of an adopted scheme.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses Geddes, naming committees, Admiral-class names, and the Nelson and Rodney paperwork.

segments.title: Ship names as Royal Navy strategic messaging
segments.summary: Admiral names could project a globally legible history of naval victory, while saint names clashed with Royal Navy cultural preferences.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that a famous admiral gave a capital ship a more pointed public message than a saint: it invoked a recognisable record of British naval power. He also frames the service's discomfort with saint names as concern over French or Spanish ecclesiastical associations, while Greek myth supplied a more familiar alternative naming reservoir.

evidence.note: The transcript compares Nelson with Saint George and explains the cultural objections to saint names.

segments.title: How a naming story can harden into fact
segments.summary: A personal note about a political conversation is weak evidence, especially once later writers repeat it as a settled fact.
segments.body: This section is a compact lesson in source criticism. The earliest claim Dr. Clarke could trace is a 1960s book, and the supposed primary evidence is a politician's note about a conversation with Geddes rather than an Admiralty naming decision; repetition can turn such a fragment into an apparently certain tradition.

evidence.note: The speaker describes the personal-note source and the chain of repeated secondary claims.

segments.title: Naval names project national history
segments.summary: Japanese, American, and British naming choices each connected warships to a national story, but the Royal Navy had established victories and admirals to display.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke compares Japanese mythological naming and American use of state names and the original six frigates with the Royal Navy's different position. Rather than inventing a new heritage, British capital ships could invoke recognised admirals and past victories, using the ship's name as psychological and diplomatic theatre before any battle.

evidence.note: The transcript compares national naming practices and connects British admiral names to prestige and deterrent messaging.

segments.title: What evidence would settle the Saint-name question
segments.summary: An Admiralty record or naming-committee source could change the conclusion; the known note mentions Saint George and Saint David, not a Saint class.
segments.body: The conclusion remains deliberately provisional: absence of evidence is not proof that no proposal existed. But the evidence described in the video attributes only Saint George and Saint David to Geddes, with no mention of Saint Patrick, Saint Andrew, or a complete Saint-class scheme, so stronger archival documentation is needed.

evidence.note: The speaker states what primary evidence would be persuasive and limits the reported names in the existing note.
